original_abstract: This chapter assesses multiple lines of evidence to evaluate past, present and future changes in the global water cycle. It complements material in Chapters 2, 3, and 4 on observed and projected changes in the water cycle, and Chapters 10 and 11 on regional climate change and extreme events. The assessment includes the physical basis for water cycle changes, observed changes in the water cycle and attribution of their causes, future projections and related key uncertainties, and the potential for abrupt change. Paleoclimate evidence, observations, reanalyses and global and regional model simulations are considered. The assessment shows widespread, non-uniform human-caused alterations of the water cycle, which have been obscured by a competition between different drivers across the 20th century that will be increasingly dominated by greenhouse gas (GHG) forcing at the global scale.
humanized_abstract: This chapter synthesizes the various lines of evidence to assess past, current and future changes of the global water cycle. It provides additional information to that presented in Chapters 2, 3 and 4 on changes in the water cycle as they have been observed and simulated and in Chapters 10 and 11 on regional aspects of climate change and extremes. The physical basis for changes in the water cycle, the observed changes, their attributions, as well as projected changes and their key sources of uncertainty and the possibility of abrupt changes are examined by synthesis of paleoclimate evidence, observations, reanalyses and global and regional model simulations. This Synthesis and Assessment Product (SAP) provides a summary of the current knowledge of water cycle changes caused by anthropogenic factors and indicates that the current changes are widespread but not uniform. Such changes were probably masked by competition between anthropogenic and natural factors during the 20th century but the increasing influence of GHG warming at the global scale should now make the anthropogenic component more apparent.

The physical basis for changes in the water cycle, the observed changes, their attributions, as well as projected changes and their key sources of uncertainty and the possibility of abrupt changes are examined by synthesis of paleoclimate evidence, observations, reanalyses and global and regional model simulations. This Synthesis and Assessment Product (SAP) provides a summary of the current knowledge of water cycle changes caused by anthropogenic factors and indicates that the current changes are widespread but not uniform. Such changes were probably masked by competition between anthropogenic and natural factors during the 20th century but the increasing influence of GHG warming at the global scale should now make the anthropogenic component more apparent.
